title: When More Is Not Better: Overcoming America's Obsession with Economic Efficiency
author: Roger L. Martin

chapters.title: Real lives behind economic theory
chapters.text:
Picture your child's kindergarten teacher — call her Sarah — waiting tables at a local restaurant, trays balanced, master's degree in hand, shaping young minds in Charlotte by day yet still living paycheck to paycheck. Sarah is not an outlier. Respectable jobs no longer guarantee stability; many workers stack second jobs just to cover basics.

Over decades the economy sorted people into a fortunate minority and a struggling majority. It became a mechanism the savvy could exploit, widening imbalance while neighbors fought poverty instead of building wealth.

Awareness is the first step to change.

Sarah's story is not only personal finance; it signals shifted rules. Viewing the economy as an instrument to enlarge the finances of a handful of people doesn't make the world a better place. Recognizing constant evolution invites broader participation and better outcomes for CEOs, educators, policymakers, and citizens alike.

An advanced economy depends on the efforts of a whole nation.

Let Sarah — and millions like her — motivate active work toward a fairer, thriving society. Embrace the challenge and begin your journey to make the world a better place.

chapters.title: Democracy and capitalism on a knife edge
chapters.text:
For generations many treated the economy as a predictable machine. The Long Depression and Great Depression reinforced that belief. Agencies like the Federal Reserve Board and Congressional Budget Office fortified the idea that economic behavior could be predicted and guided.

Roger L. Martin's team at the Martin Prosperity Institute studied democratic capitalism while excluding the top ten percent of earners. Surveying households between $25,000 and $110,000, they found deep disconnect and dissatisfaction.

Conversations across occupations revealed a pattern: the economy felt adversarial. Political engagement thinned — politics seemed an endless stalemate with little getting done. People knew they should participate yet doubted impact.

Democracy thrives when every citizen feels seen and heard.

Americans long backed capitalism because it lifted most incomes. That bargain has frayed in recent years. If capitalism appears to serve only elites, alternatives gain appeal and the current order weakens.

We shape our buildings, and afterward our buildings shape us. ~ Winston Churchill

Historical nuance matters. The Great Depression devastated banks and jobs, yet middle-income pain today has persisted longer in some respects. During the Depression the wealthy absorbed a larger share of losses than average workers. By the late 1980s, poorer Americans often gained most from growth.

Balance, not perfection, is the cornerstone of a strong economy.

Chasing a flawless economic machine invites fragility and exploitation. Sustainable progress favors steady improvement over utopian precision.

chapters.title: Models that shape American capitalism
chapters.text:
Capitalism places most resources in private hands, allocated by supply and demand. Several models frame business and policy. The customer-loyalty model pushes retention over endless acquisition. The educational cause-and-effect model ties school funding to measurable student outcomes since tax dollars significantly fund them.

At the federal level, Otto Eckstein's computer forecasting model lets Congress estimate fiscal impacts — another machine metaphor shared by many businesses.

Mathematics enters through Carl Friedrich Gauss's bell curve, describing everything from human height to manufacturing variance. It underpins expectations of a broad middle class with steadily rising incomes.

John Sterman notes every model simplifies reality, projecting some forces while ignoring others. Still, we always operate from some model, often unconsciously.

Models provide a simplified version of things, but the reality is always more complex.

Over-reliance carries risk. Vilfredo Pareto observed that twenty percent of Italian families owned eighty percent of land — a skew that echoes modern CEO pay versus stagnant manager wages. Giants like Facebook and Amazon pursue dominance that helps owners short-term while hollowing out smaller competitors.

Understanding the whole system — not just efficiency or market share — is essential. Acknowledging skew and aiming for inclusivity can spread opportunity and build a healthier community.

chapters.title: The economy as a living system
chapters.text:
Popular discourse treats the American economy like a predictable machine. Martin argues it behaves more like an evolving natural system — dynamic, interactive, and adaptive rather than mechanically fixed.

Grasping an economy resembles understanding a family: individual parts matter less than relationships among them. Collective interactions, not isolated components, define outcomes.

Complexity demands adaptability, not rigidity.

Toys R Us and iHeartMedia illustrate the point. Both titans faltered not simply from mismanagement but from leveraged-buyout ownership that loaded them with massive debts despite solid operations. When industry conditions shifted, crushing liabilities proved fatal.

Natural economic systems share three traits:

• Complexity
• Adaptability
• Systemic structure

Honoring those qualities lets the economy flourish without heavy-handed control.

Harnessing democratic capitalism requires equilibrium — balancing efficiency against resilience. Over-index on resilience without efficiency and competitors outpace you; chase efficiency without stability and shocks trigger collapse.

Balancing efficiency and resilience is the cornerstone of sustainable progress.

Did you know? According to a Facts.net article from September 2023, California's best assets are people and companies. It is home to some of the world's most valuable companies, such as Apple, Facebook, Walt Disney, Chevron, and Wells Fargo. California's economy is the biggest in the United States, recording a $3,598 trillion gross state product as of 2022.

chapters.title: Wisdom from business and government
chapters.text:
Executive choices ripple beyond firms into the wider economy. Joe's Stone Crab in Miami Beach ranks among America's top-grossing independent restaurants despite closing months each year when stone crabs are out of season. Long-term vision and resilient practices — not relentless efficiency — sustain that success. Leaders should view the economy as organic, not mechanical.

Sustainable success lies in long-term vision and adaptability.

Politics needs forward-looking policies grounded in public experience. Empathy improves legislation.

Canada's financial regulation offers contrast. Where the U.S. often legislates reactively, Canada emphasizes relationships. During the global financial crisis, OSFI Superintendent Julie Dickson met major bank CEOs regularly, blending informal oversight with capital rules and activity limits. Daily supervisory management is equally vital.

The Bank Act undergoes formal review every five years — a 1992 reform keeping finance responsive without perpetual political warfare.

European tenure-based voting — used in France, Italy, and Belgium — grants extra shareholder votes for long-held stock, privileging patient capital over short-term trading. Long-term capital is far more valuable for a company's long-term strategy than short-term capital.

In a fluctuating world, flexible policies are the cornerstone of stability.

American leaders in business and government must cultivate economic intelligence: embrace complexity, champion stability, and collaborate across sectors for a more balanced future.

chapters.title: Educators and citizens as change agents
chapters.text:
At Central Elementary School in Hamilton, Ontario — founded 1853 — principal Beth Grosso leads a diverse student body, many learning English as a second language. She models adaptability, urging students to challenge thoughts, gain feedback, and refine ideas. That approach prepares minds for flux rather than rote memorization.

Unified voices can shift the mightiest tides.

Education often silos disciplines for instructor convenience, teaching students to decompose problems into isolated parts. Finance, performance management, and budgeting appear separate though deeply linked.

Schools must help learners see a complex, changing world — not a pile of independent modules. Educators must mold adaptable, questioning thinkers.

A positive future for American democratic capitalism needs contribution from everyone. ~ Roger L. Martin

Citizen action moves markets too. When limited buyers threatened Canadian tomato growers, collective response opened new channels — proof that purchasing and organizing shift power.

Every purchase, every vote, echoes in the vast halls of democracy.

Citizens can reshape democratic capitalism by:

• Exercising purchasing power: every dollar spent is a declaration of values.
• Engaging in collective action: pooled voices resonate louder in market and political arenas.
• Demanding reciprocal politics: leaders should echo public sentiment with mutual benefits.
• Voting with intent: informed ballots align policy with citizen priorities.

Start locally. Small, persistent steps catalyze broader balance.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
America stands at a fork for democratic capitalism. One path deepens the obsession with narrow efficiency; the other embraces balance and adaptability.

For more than 250 years democratic capitalism fueled growth, yet the machine model is overdue for revision. Treating the economy as a complex adaptive system can harmonize efficiency with resilience.

No single group owns the future. Corporate leaders, educators, policymakers, and citizens must collaborate. Their adaptability, commitment, and drive for collaboration will be pivotal.

Passive observation is not an option. Hope and concern both rest in collective action. Together we can build a resilient America rooted in balanced democratic capitalism. Let's embrace the challenge and shape our future.

Try this

• Support local businesses to strengthen economic resilience.
• Engage in political discourse and attend community events.
• Ask candidates directly how they will pursue balanced growth.
• Dedicate time to educate yourself about the financial system and its evolving dynamics.

Read it till the end?
